b  Due to its inherent advantages of immutability, secu-
e  rity, and trustworthiness, blockchain technology is widely
in
regarded as possessing  the potential  to serve as  the
s.
foundational  infrastructure for  the realization of Web
al
y-  3.0 [2],  [3]. However, despite the promising potential
h  of blockchain  technology,  there exist  two significant
of  challenges that must be addressed for successful  real-
world implementation. The first  challenge pertains  to
g
the inherent  limitations of  the blockchain architecture.
p-
al  Traditionally, blockchain is only considered as a one-
al  dimensional  data structure and has  limited reflection
ss  of  the  actual world  [4],  although  there have been
ys
some structural innovations in public networks, such as
c-
sidechains [5], directed acyclic graphs (DAG) [6] and
d
h  Layer 2 networks [7], these solutions are predominantly
non-deterministic in nature, resulting in low throughput
s  and slow consensus formation, and some carefully de-
signed deterministic permissioned blockchain networks,
such as hyperledger [8], R3 [9]and Consensys [10], suffer
from limited network extensibility and are unable to be
al
scaled to large-scale applications. The second challenge
e
is closely related to the complexity of communication.
k
Traditional proof-based consensus algorithms,  such as
a
Proof-of-Work (PoW)  [11], which  is well  known  to
r,
require substantial computational resources [12], while
6,
Proof-of-Stake (PoS) [13] tends to result  in monopoly
ss
issues,  limiting decentralization and fairness [14]. The
s
existing  lighter  consensus algorithms,  such as PBFT
to
[15], Paxos [16] and Raft [16] don’t perform ideal ei-
d
ther, because they all exhibit polynomial communication
complexity,  thus being  limited  to achieve  large-scale
extensibility and scalability.
ch
ng  The primary objective of  this work  is  to address
he
those two key challenges identified by proposing a novel
e
is  blockchain topology structure, along with the design of
ry  an efficient consensus algorithm,  thus creating a data
ur
network that possesses inherent authenticity, spatiotem-
ex
as  poral continuity, and large-scale consistency - capabilities
that are essential  for realizing the Web 3.0 vision.  In

A. Hyper-simplex fractal topology construction
To understand the shape of a Hyper-simplex fracta
the key is to investigate how a single iteration will chang
a face.
Obviously, the face, together with the nodes adjace
to it, constructs a (N − 1)−simplex network. When a
iteration occurs, the midpoint of each edge and a distanc
out of the simplex along the normal direction outside th
simplex will generate a new node, which will genera
a total of N new nodes per face. Sometimes, the node
generated by two adjacent faces will appear in the sam
position, and they need to be treated as different node
Fig. 1: 4-simplex fractals

m

ts  is approximately

n.
-  Fractals themselves are geometric structures that ex-
e  hibit scale invariance, and this property also extends to
al  the representation of nodes within a fractal network.
],  Here, we introduce the subscript pair representation to
y,  represent nodes, while address mapping serves as a direct
p  translation of these indexed pairs.
s,  By utilizing duality, we can alternately and recursively
e  represent each node and face within the fractal network,
ty  and the number of subscript pairs in the subscript rep-
er  resents which tier it  is generated in. The detailed rep-
d  resentation methods can be found in the corresponding

s.  Hyper-simplex  fractal  network works  similarly of
label-based routing and switching,  e.g., Multiprotocol
Label Switching (MPLS) [26], and take the principles
of ”Switch where possible,  route if necessary”. Upon
the entry of  the Hyper-simplex fractals network edge,
the Hyper-simplex fractals network locator  is used to
create the pseudo-deterministic yet robust paths before
dispatching the flow. The label will be generated at the
time of connecting to any part of the network, and it will

also carry the deterministic routes using the tier numb
locator.
The detailed label-making process is dependent on th
size of  the number of  top-level nodes N and the ti
number m. The subscript pair representation of a nod
exactly reflects the iter position of the node in Hype
simplex fractals network; for each subscript pair in th
subscript pair representation, ⌈log2 N⌉+1 bits are neede
to represent, the first ⌈log2 N⌉ bits represent the first dig
of the subscript pair, which ranges from 1 to N, and th
other bit represents the second digit of the subscript pa
which can be 0 or 1. To determine the number N, a
additional ⌈log2 N⌉ bits are needed. So totally
m(⌈log2 N⌉ + 1) + ⌈log2 N⌉ = (m+ 1)⌈log2 N⌉ +m
(II.2
bits are needed to determine  the dimension and it
number of one Hyper-simplex fractal network and
be a  tier  locator of KN,m. When the subscript pa
is not  long enough,  all  unset  numbers are set  to
because the last subscript of an existing node can n
be (k, 1), 1 ≤ k ≤ N. Thus, we can locate the last zer
in the tier locator to determine which iteration the nod
is generated in.
Let VN,m be the node set of N−simplex fractal wi
tier m. For example, v(2,0)’s tier locator in V3,3 is
10 : 010 : 111 : 111
where the first 10 means N = 2 + 1 = 3 and the
are 3 sets of numbers behind so m = 3. Similarl
v(1,0),(2,1),(3,0)’s tier locator in V3,3 is
10 : 000 : 011 : 100
If a node’s tier locator is
11 : 000 : 011 : 100 : 111
It will be v(1,0),(2,1),(3,0)  in V4,4 because the first 1
means N = 3 + 1 = 4 and there are 4 sets of numbe
behind so m = 4, and the last zero in its tier locato
appears in the fourth set of numbers means this node
generated in the third iteration.
C. Consensus tree and hierarchical consensus mecha
nism
We can consider all  the nodes of  the same sma
simplex as a whole, and with the help of the conce
of mathematical quotient mapping [27],  reorganize th
nodes on a Hyper-simplex fractal network. All the node
of the same small simplex will form a consensus nod
and all the consensus nodes will be organized as a tre

er  which we call a consensus tree. For a N-simplex fractal,
there is only one consensus node in the lowest  layer,
k−2
e  while the k-th layer contains a total of N(2N − 2)
er  consensus nodes. The consensus node at  the  lowest
e  layer directly leads N consensus nodes, whereas each of
r-  the other non-top-layer consensus nodes directly leads
e  2(N − 1) consensus nodes.
d  A Multi-Layer PBFT consensus  is operated on the
it  Hyper-simplex fractal network.

In one consensus node, a
e  traditional PBFT [15] consensus is operated, and in this
ir,  process, they will also vote a leader node to reply to the
n  result to each node in its father consensus node, forming
the downward propagation of  consensus,  and  repeat
this process until it reaches the lowest consensus node.
Similarly, once the father consensus node has formed a
)
consensus, its leader node will send the message to each
er
node of all of its directly direct successor nodes except
to
for the message source.

it  is also necessary to design the update order of  the
)  space-time blockchain, which resembles an Ouroboros-
like update order.
el  By constructing a  loop that  traverses all nodes of
f-  the Hyper-simplex fractal network, where nodes within
h  the same consensus subtree are adjacent in the traversal
s.  order, we can propose a feasible update sequence for
n  the space-time blockchain. The design of this traversal
n  loop is somewhat like a depth-first search. In fact, after
introducing the concept of consensus trees, this traversal
loop is indeed a depth-first search on consensus trees.
Fig. 8: Node traversal order (the color of each face is the
average color of its vertices).

In practical  applications,  it  is possible  for  certain
nodes  to be empty or  faulty.  In such cases,  during
the update process of the space-time blockchain,  these
nodes can simply be skipped temporarily. When the
d  number of nodes  is very large,  the waiting time for
ot  update intervals may be lengthy.  In such cases,  it  is

Fig. 9: The  traversal order corresponds  to depth-fir
search on the consensus tree.
necessary to implement some optimization strategies, lik
region partitioning, loop nesting, etc, even at the cost o
sacrificing some synchronization.
F. Security analysis and fault tolerance evaluation
There are two main malicious attack models in di
tributed systems and blockchain:  the faulty probabili
determined (FPD) model and the faulty number dete
mined (FND) model [28], [29], used when the probabili
of every single faulty node is fixed and the number o
faulty nodes in the system is fixed respectively. For FPD
the failure probability of each node is independent, an
the number of failed nodes within a certain range follow
a binomial distribution. In contrast, for FNP, since the to
tal number of failed nodes is fixed, the failure probabili
of each node is dependent on whether other nodes hav
failed, and the number of failed nodes within a certa
range follows a hypergeometric distribution.

Howeve
when the number of nodes is large, the hypergeometr
distribution approximates the binomial distribution [30
so the results for FND are similar  to those for FPD
Our simulation experiments also support this observatio
Since  the Hyper-simplex fractal network is primari
used in large-scale scenarios, we will mainly focus o
safety analysis for FPD in this section.

as Pf  and the consensus success  rate of N-simple
m

if and only if its lowest layer consensus node function
properly. For each consensus node,  it can operate no

G. Dynamic Node Positioning Based on Node Perfor-
,
mance
d
The  fractal  network  topology  inherently creates  a
s
hierarchical  structure where nodes at  different  levels
-
face varying demands and responsibilities. We propose
ty
a dynamic node positioning system that  leverages this
e
characteristic to create a truly peer-to-peer network by
in
adjusting node positions based on their demonstrated
r,
overall performance.
ic
],  In this system, nodes are not permanently fixed to
.  their initial positions within the network. Instead,  their
n.  placement is fluid and determined by their performance
ly  metrics. The fundamental principle is straightforward:
n  nodes  that exhibit  superior performance are promoted
to higher levels within the fractal structure, while those
with lesser capabilities may be moved to lower  levels
d
or expelled from the consensus group,  i.e.,  losing the
x
vertices.
er
This approach offers several advantages:
ly
s  1) Optimal Resource Utilization: By positioning
r-  high-performance nodes at  higher  levels where
d  they handle more critical  tasks,  the network can
s  more efficiently utilize its resources.
e  2) Natural Load Balancing: As stronger nodes as-
cend the hierarchy,  they naturally take on more.

Our work  introduces a novel mathematical  fram
work that unifies fractal geometry,  topology, network
ing science, blockchain data structure, and distribute
consensus to revolutionize blockchain network desig
This approach not  only solves critical  challenges
blockchain scalability and efficiency but also provide
a powerful  toolkit  for understanding adaptive networ
behaviors across diverse fields. By bridging abstra
mathematics with practical engineering, we demonstra
how interdisciplinary research can lead to breakthroug
solutions in network science.
Future work will focus on refining these models fo
broader applications in social and urban networks, po
tentially transforming our approach to complex system
design and analysis. This research opens new avenue
for exploring the fundamental principles governing larg
scale, dynamic networks of blockchain, promising signi
icant advancements in both theoretical mathematics an
practical network engineering.
